excerpt: The Prophet's charge that ʿAlī would fight for the taʾwīl of the Qurʾān as the Prophet had fought for its tanzīl is a recurrent motif in the Fatimid taʾwīl tradition, anchoring the doctrine that the outward conflicts of ʿAlī's caliphate were themselves struggles over the inner meaning of revelation.
source_location_hint: Introductory sections on the taʾwīl of the Qurʾān and the rank of the waṣī

excerpt: ʿAlī's own sermons articulate the principle that the divine One is not 'one' as members of a numerical sequence are 'one': number applies only to that which admits division and limit, whereas the Originator transcends both. He is described as 'aḥad' without being countable, 'wāḥid' without being a unit in a series.
source_author: al-Sharīf al-Raḍī (compiler); Imām ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib
source_authority: Imām ʿAlī, as preserved by al-Sharīf al-Raḍī
source_location_hint: Khuṭba 152 (Khuṭbat al-Ashbāḥ) and the opening Khuṭba on tawḥīd

excerpt: The teaching that the Qurʾān bears an outward (ẓāhir) form of words and an inward (bāṭin) form of meaning, and that the Imām from the family of the Prophet is the bearer of that inward meaning, is the structural premise of Fatimid taʾwīl. Each Qurʾānic āya is treated as a sign pointing back to the Imām who actualises its meaning in the daʿwa.
source_location_hint: Opening chapters on the relation of tanzīl to taʾwīl and the rank of the waṣī

excerpt: ʿAlī teaches in his opening sermon that God dispatched the prophets to the human race so that they might 'demand of them the fulfilment of the covenant of His creation, remind them of His forgotten bounty, present to them the argument by way of preaching, and stir up for them the buried treasures of the intellects (yuthīrū lahum dafāʾin al-ʿuqūl).'
source_author: al-Sharīf al-Raḍī (compiler); Imām ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib
source_authority: Imām ʿAlī, as preserved by al-Sharīf al-Raḍī
source_location_hint: Khuṭba 1 (opening sermon on creation and prophethood)

excerpt: This articulation — that the very negation of an attribute must itself be negated, lest 'negation' be made into a name of the divine essence — is the doctrine of double tanzīh worked out most rigorously by Abū Yaʿqūb al-Sijistānī, who teaches that the Originator is not to be described either by an attribute or by its negation, since both belong to the order of created things.
source_location_hint: on the doctrine of tanzīh and the negation of negation

excerpt: Al-Sijistānī treats the divine attributes as identical with the essence, distinguished from one another only through the operation of the conceiving mind: they are not adjuncts adhering to a substrate but the essence itself viewed under different aspects.
source_location_hint: on the unity of the divine essence and its attributes

excerpt: The Imam's lament that he could find no fit bearer for the knowledge in his breast is taken up in Fatimid daʿwa literature as a charter for the carefully graded transmission of taʾwīl, pledged from one trustworthy dāʿī to another rather than scattered indiscriminately. Qāḍī al-Nuʿmān reflects this discipline in his treatment of the imamate.
source_location_hint: Kitab al-Walaya, on the transmission and custody of esoteric knowledge

excerpt: The doctrine that the earth is never devoid of a qāʾim bi-ḥujjat Allāh — one who upholds God's proof in every age, whether manifest or concealed — is the bedrock of Fatimid imamology. Qāḍī al-Nuʿmān cites this saying as a foundational warrant for the doctrine of the perpetual imamate.
source_location_hint: Kitab al-Walaya, on the necessity of a hujja in every age

excerpt: هَجَمَ بِهِمُ الْعِلْمُ عَلَى حَقِيقَةِ الْبَصِيرَةِ — Through them, knowledge has stormed the very reality of insight.
source_location_hint: Hikam (Sayings), within the celebrated discourse to Kumayl ibn Ziyad on the ranks of hearts and the bearers of true knowledge

excerpt: The wise man fights against his self through knowledge; the most powerful is the one who stands against his own self; no jihad is greater than the jihad of the self.
source_location_hint: Khutab and Hikam treating jihad al-nafs and the inward war against caprice (hawā)

excerpt: Shall I not inform you of the true faqīh? He is the one who does not cause people to despair of the mercy of Allāh, does not make them lose hope in the relief of Allāh, and does not give them immunity from the chastisement of Allāh.
source_location_hint: Ḥikam (Short Sayings), Saying 90

excerpt: The perfection of the intellect (kamāl al-ʿaql) in the soul is not realised except through the disposition of the noble virtues, for these virtues are the very form in which intellect actualises itself in the human being.
source_location_hint: On the relation of moral disposition (akhlāq) to the ascent of the soul toward al-ʿAql

excerpt: Worship without sincerity (ikhlāṣ) does not ascend to Allāh; for the form of the act unaccompanied by its inner intent is as a body without a soul, and Allāh does not accept from His servants but what is offered purely for His sake.
source_location_hint: Majālis on the inward meaning of worship and the centrality of ikhlāṣ

excerpt: As for the God-fearing (al-muttaqūn), they are the people of virtue in this world: their speech is right, their dress is moderate, and their gait is humble. They have lowered their gaze before what Allāh has forbidden for them, and they have given their hearing to the knowledge that benefits them. Their souls are with those who are tried just as they are with those who are at ease.
source_location_hint: Khuṭbat al-Muttaqīn (Sermon of the God-fearing), also known as Khuṭbat Hammām, Sermon 193

excerpt: The soul, when it has been purified by knowledge and dispossessed of the appetites of the body, attains in this world to a foretaste of the world to come; for the Garden of the spirit is not other than the soul's own felicity in the contemplation of its Originator.
source_location_hint: Yanbūʿ on the soul's perfection and its anticipation of the spiritual Paradise

excerpt: Wallāhi la-ibnu Abī Ṭālibin ānasu bi-l-mawti min al-ṭifli bi-thadyi ummihi
source_location_hint: Khuṭba 5 — sermon delivered after the death of the Prophet

excerpt: law kushifa al-ghiṭāʾ mā izdadtu yaqīnan
source_location_hint: saying widely transmitted from ʿAlī in the Shīʿī corpus; among the ḥikam

excerpt: ʿAhd al-Ashtar — the covenant of governance for Egypt addressed to Mālik al-Ashtar
source_location_hint: Letter (Kitāb) 53 — the testament to Mālik al-Ashtar on assuming the governorship of Egypt

excerpt: al-ʿadlu yaḍaʿu al-umūra mawāḍiʿahā
source_location_hint: Ḥikam (sayings), in answer to which is preferable, justice or generosity

excerpt: on ʿadl and the duties of the imām toward God's creation, distinguishing justice as the binding sum of the cardinal virtues
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Walāya — opening discussions on the imāma and ʿadl

excerpt: This is what the servant of God, ʿAlī, Amīr al-Muʾminīn, ordered Mālik ibn al-Ḥārith al-Ashtar in his instrument of appointment when he made him governor of Egypt
source_location_hint: Letter 53 (ʿAhd to Malik al-Ashtar), opening

excerpt: Habituate your heart to mercy for the subjects ... you stand above them, and the commander of the affair stands above you, and God stands above the one who appointed you.
source_location_hint: Kitab al-Jihad / on the duties of rulers and the rights of subjects

excerpt: Know that the people are constituted of classes, none of which prospers save through the others ... For each of these God has fixed a share
source_location_hint: On the ordered ranks of the Muslim polity (kharaj, jizya, judges, army)

excerpt: Al-Sijistānī links obedience to the Imam and to the divine command with the soul's release from its own bondage, arguing that the lower self is bound until the light of guidance descends upon it.
source_location_hint: Yanbūʿ on the soul and obedience

excerpt: Nāṣir-i Khusraw teaches that the soul's return (rujūʿ) to its origin is the completion of the very fiṭra in which it was first inscribed; the soul, having issued from the world of command, finds its rest only in returning to that source.
source_location_hint: discussion of fiṭra and the soul's return

excerpt: Qāḍī al-Nuʿmān explains that the prophets and their legatees were sent precisely to draw forth from the souls the hidden deposits of knowledge entrusted to them at the primordial covenant, so that what was latent might be actualised by the daʿwa.
source_location_hint: taʾwīl of the primordial covenant

excerpt: Al-Kirmānī holds that the believer's perfection lies in conforming the soul to the divine attributes through knowledge and act, so that the attributes become the very substance of the soul's worship.
source_location_hint: on the cultivation of divine attributes in the believer

excerpt: Al-Muʾayyad fī al-Dīn al-Shīrāzī teaches that the soul cannot of itself ascend to truth; every motion toward the good is itself a gift drawn down by the daʿwa and the grace of the Imam of the age.
source_location_hint: majālis on grace and the soul's striving

excerpt: Qāḍī al-Nuʿmān, expounding the inner sense of the pillars, frames justice (ʿadl) as the soul's verification of the Real (taḥqīq al-ḥaqq) rather than the bare execution of statute, so that to act justly is to make the truth manifest in being.
source_location_hint: taʾwīl of ʿadl and the inward dimension of the pillars

excerpt: Al-Sijistānī argues that pride (kibr) is the inward form of shirk, for the proud man arrogates to himself the attribute al-Mutakabbir which belongs to God alone, and so contends with the very oneness (waḥdāniyya) he is summoned to confess.
source_location_hint: chapter on the names and the vice of arrogance

excerpt: In his sermon known as al-Qāṣiʿa, ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib portrays Iblīs as the imām of the bigoted and the progenitor of every arrogant tyrant, and counsels the believer to clothe himself in the garment of humility as a fortress against satanic suggestion.
source_authority: compilation of ʿAlī's sermons and letters (d. 406/1015)
source_location_hint: Sermon al-Qāṣiʿa on arrogance and Iblīs

excerpt: Imam Zayn al-ʿĀbidīn supplicates against the deception of self-admiration, asking that no act of obedience be so adorned in his eyes that pride enter into it, and that no sin be so disguised that he despair of repentance.
source_location_hint: supplication on noble moral traits (makārim al-akhlāq)

excerpt: Take warning from what God did to Iblīs when his prolonged worship and exhaustive striving did not avail him, for he refused a single prostration on account of his pride; thus did one moment of arrogance undo an age of obedience.
source_location_hint: Sermon 192 (Khuṭbat al-Qāṣiʿa)

excerpt: Deeds are by intentions, and to each person belongs only what he has intended; the outward act is rendered valid or void by the inner niyya alone.
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Ṭahāra / Bāb al-Niyya

excerpt: Let the dearest of your affairs be those that hold to the middle course in truth, are the most comprehensive in justice, and the most embracing of the contentment of the commonalty; for the displeasure of the commonalty undoes the contentment of the elite, while the displeasure of the elite is overlooked when the commonalty is content.
source_location_hint: Letter 53 (to Malik al-Ashtar, governor of Egypt)

excerpt: God has imposed upon the imams of justice that they keep themselves at the level of the weakest of people, so that the poor may not be agitated by their own poverty.
source_location_hint: Sermon 209 (the encounter with ʿĀṣim ibn Ziyād al-Ḥārithī)

excerpt: Whoever shows mercy is shown mercy; and I marvel at one who hopes for mercy from above yet withholds it from below.
source_location_hint: short sayings / aphorisms (qiṣār al-ḥikam)

excerpt: Letter to Mālik al-Ashtar instructing the governor of Egypt on the discipline of corrupt officials and the proper administration of justice.
source_location_hint: Letter 53 (ʿAhd al-Ashtar)

excerpt: By God, this world of yours is to me lower than the substance that flows from a goat's nostril — were it not for the covenant God took from the learned not to acquiesce in the gluttony of the oppressor nor in the hunger of the oppressed.
source_location_hint: Sermon 3 (al-Shaqshaqiyya)

excerpt: Do not include among your counsellors a miser, a coward, or a greedy man, for each of these arises from an ill opinion of God.
source_location_hint: Letter 53 (ʿAhd al-Ashtar), section on the selection of ministers

excerpt: O God, I am the poor in my wealth, so how should I not be poor in my poverty? ... You are the Self-sufficient, and we are the indigent before You.
source_author: Imām Zayn al-ʿĀbidīn ʿAlī b. al-Ḥusayn
source_location_hint: Munājāt al-Iftiqār (Whispered Prayer of the Indigent) and related supplications on dependence

excerpt: The differentiated orders of the polity — armies, scribes, judiciary, officers, tax-collectors, peasants, merchants, artisans, and the destitute — each with rights upon the ruler, who himself stands under a prior covenant to God.
source_authority: sayings of Imam ʿAlī b. Abī Ṭālib
source_location_hint: Letter 53 (to Mālik al-Ashtar al-Nakhaʿī upon his appointment to Egypt), opening section on the classes of the polity

excerpt: The judge must be one whom intricate cases do not confound, nor the persistence of litigants exasperate; firm in the truth, undeceived by surface arguments, generously provided for so as to need nothing from the people.
source_authority: sayings of Imam ʿAlī b. Abī Ṭālib
source_location_hint: Letter 53, the section on the qualifications of judges (qaḍāʾ)

excerpt: Direct God-given charge to the ruler concerning the lower orders — the poor, the orphans, the elderly, and the disabled — whose case must be brought before him by a dedicated officer.
source_authority: sayings of Imam ʿAlī b. Abī Ṭālib
source_location_hint: Letter 53, the section on the lowest class (al-ṭabaqa al-suflā) of the destitute and the disabled

excerpt: A group worships God out of desire — and that is the worship of merchants; a group worships out of fear — and that is the worship of slaves; a group worships out of gratitude — and that is the worship of the free.
source_authority: sayings of Imam ʿAlī b. Abī Ṭālib
source_location_hint: Ḥikam (short sayings / aphorisms) section, on the three classes of worshippers

excerpt: The verses of Sūrat al-Insān (76:8–9) are read by the Imāmī and Ismāʿīlī tradition as having been revealed in honour of Mawlānā ʿAlī and his family in connection with their fasting and feeding of the poor man, the orphan, and the captive — the paradigmatic instance of action performed purely for the face of God.
source_location_hint: Sections expounding Quranic verses traditionally read as descending in the merit of the Ahl al-Bayt, particularly concerning charity and intention

excerpt: Imam ʿAlī's recourse to the Quranic promise that taqwā opens a way out from trials and a light amid darkness, deployed as the spiritual ground enabling the just ruler to persist through the failures of his outward caliphate.
source_authority: sayings of Imam ʿAlī b. Abī Ṭālib
source_location_hint: Sayings and sermons reflecting on taqwā as the path through fitna; resonant with Q. 65:2–3

excerpt: And know that you are charged with affairs whose success is conditioned upon nothing so much as the obligatory acts of worship rendered to God. So give to God a portion of yourself, in your nights and your days, and fulfil completely those acts by which you draw near to God, performing them without flaw or defect, to the limit of your body's endurance.
source_location_hint: Letter 53 (to Malik al-Ashtar al-Nakhaʿi, governor of Egypt)

excerpt: Indeed God, glorified be He, has made remembrance a polish for hearts, by which they hear after their deafness, see after their blindness, and become tractable after their defiance.
source_location_hint: Sermon 222 (on the virtue of remembrance and the people of dhikr)

excerpt: Eyes do not perceive Him by the sight of vision, but hearts perceive Him by the realities of faith.
source_location_hint: Sermon 179 (in answer to Dhiʿlab al-Yamani's question)

excerpt: the work of dhikr and recitation as the means by which the soul receives the impressions of the higher world and translates intellectual realities into a settled condition of the heart
source_location_hint: sections treating dhikr, recitation, and the soul's reception of higher impressions

excerpt: the heart as the seat of insight whose purification by dhikr joins inner light with outward act of remembrance
source_location_hint: discussion of the conditions of spiritual knowledge and the inner/outer dimensions of recollection

excerpt: God known without being seen, manifest in creation yet beyond the grasp of created intellects
source_author: al-Sharif al-Radi (compiler), sayings of Imam ʿAli
source_location_hint: opening khutba on the divine attributes

excerpt: to stir up for them the buried treasures of intellects (dafaʾin al-ʿuqul)
source_author: al-Sharif al-Radi (compiler), sayings of Imam ʿAli
source_location_hint: Sermon 1 (al-Khutba al-Ula) on the mission of the prophets

excerpt: the inner sight of tawhid harmonises apparent opposites — zahir and batin, transcendence and proximity — under the single light of the Real
source_location_hint: majalis discussing the unity of zahir and batin and the vision of tawhid

excerpt: إن الله سبحانه جعل الذكر جلاءً للقلوب، تسمع به بعد الوقرة، وتبصر به بعد العشوة، وتنقاد به بعد المعاندة
source_location_hint: Khutba 222 (on the virtue of dhikr and those who remember God)

excerpt: The reciprocity of love, remembrance, and contemplation as a mutually reinforcing ascent is a central motif of Fatimid spiritual writing.
source_location_hint: Yanbuʿ on the ranks of the worshippers and the ascent of the soul through dhikr

excerpt: The Imams of the ahl al-bayt are reported to have maintained the remembrance of God in every state — at meals, on the road, and at rest — exemplifying the principle that dhikr alone has no terminus among the prescribed acts.
source_authority: reports from Imam Jaʿfar al-Sadiq and Imam Muhammad al-Baqir
source_location_hint: Kitab al-Salat, bab fadl dhikr Allah (chapter on the virtue of the remembrance of God)

excerpt: Imam ʿAlī repeatedly identifies sincerity (ikhlāṣ) and the inward jihād against the self as the gateway to true worship, framing outward devotion without inward purification as essentially negligent.
source_location_hint: sermons and sayings on sincerity and the greater jihad

excerpt: the divine command 'Be!' (kun) treated as the originating word through which existents are summoned from non-being and remain suspended in the act of divine address
source_location_hint: discussion of the divine word kun and the procession of existents

excerpt: the perfection of sincerity (kamal al-ikhlas) lies in stripping God of attributes, since every attribute bears witness to its own otherness from the One it qualifies
source_location_hint: Sermon 1 (al-khutba al-ula)

excerpt: ʿAlī will fight for the taʾwīl of the Qurʾān as I fought for its tanzīl — a prophetic dictum invoked by Fatimid authors as the cornerstone of the imamic interpretive office.
source_location_hint: Introductory chapters on the inheritance of the taʾwīl function from the Prophet to ʿAlī and the imams

excerpt: Taʾwīl is the act of returning a thing to its awwal (origin); the etymological reading frames the entire discipline of esoteric exegesis.
source_location_hint: Opening sections on the meaning and necessity of taʾwīl

excerpt: The First Intellect (al-ʿAql al-Awwal) is the ontological prototype of prophetic revelation, and the partial intellect in the human being is its receiving correlate.
source_location_hint: Doctrine of the First Intellect and its relation to prophetic and partial intellects

excerpt: …that they might unearth for them the buried treasures of the intellects (yuthīrū lahum dafāʾin al-ʿuqūl) — the locus classicus for the prophetic mission as awakening of latent intellect.
source_author: al-Sharīf al-Raḍī (compiler), words of ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib
source_location_hint: Sermon 1 (the opening khuṭba)

excerpt: The harmony of scriptural revelation (tanzīl) with the universal intellect is the principle reconciling philosophical and prophetic knowledge; the intellect is the inner counterpart of the outer Book.
source_location_hint: Programmatic chapters on the unity of philosophical and religious knowledge

excerpt: The apophatic procedure — the double negation of attributes, and of the negation of attributes itself — is the proper method by which the intellect approaches the Originator (al-Mubdiʿ).
source_location_hint: Yanābīʿ on tawḥīd and the limits of discursive predication of the Originator

excerpt: distinction between formal knowledge (ʿilm rasmī) and the inner reality (ḥaqīqa) toward which it points
source_location_hint: discussions on the distinction between the apparent and the hidden in knowledge

excerpt: soul cannot be liberated from its own egoism except by a higher principle: the support of the daʿwa and the First Intellect
source_location_hint: sections on the soul's need for guidance and the hierarchy of intellects

excerpt: ikhlas as the precondition by which outward devotion becomes inwardly real, and the inseparability of witness from the sacrifice of the lower self
source_authority: Fatimid Ismaili daʿwa, chief dāʿī under al-Mustansir
source_location_hint: majālis on sincerity and the inward dimensions of worship
